source.title: Motivational states activate distinct hippocampal representations to guide goal-directed behaviors
source.venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences USA

confidence_rationale: Direct hippocampal electrophysiology showing that hunger vs. thirst drive states activate distinct CA1 ensemble representations of the same spatial environment during goal-directed (memory-dependent) tasks. This is strong evidence that hippocampal map nodes encode drive-state-gated valence information -- i.e., the same location node fires differently depending on which motivational state is active. Supports the drive-state-gated replay prioritisation claim in SD-014. Confidence docked slightly because the effect is measured at ensemble representation level rather than valence-vector component level; the paper does not dissect wanting vs. liking separately.
failure_signatures: If hippocampal representations were identical regardless of hunger vs. thirst state, the drive-state gating component of SD-014 valence vectors would lack hippocampal grounding
If the effect appeared equally in random foraging (non-goal-directed) as in memory-guided tasks, this would suggest the modulation is not tied to goal-directed valence encoding

mapping.source_claim_statement: Motivational state (hunger vs. thirst) differentially activates hippocampal CA1 ensemble representations when the same locations are visited during memory-guided goal-directed behavior; the effect is not present during random foraging when motivation is incidental to behavior.
mapping.ree_translation: SD-014 stipulates that replay prioritisation is weighted by drive-state-gated valence relevance. This paper shows that the hippocampal map itself is modulated by drive state: food-relevant nodes are not simply flagged by fixed valence values but by which motivational state is currently active. The w component specifically -- wanting, drive-scaled incentive salience -- must therefore be computed relative to the current drive state rather than being a fixed stored value. A hungry agent should see its food-location nodes activated more strongly than its water-location nodes, and vice versa when thirsty. This is exactly what Kennedy and Shapiro observe at the ensemble level.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The paper measures ensemble rate-map overlap (correlation across sessions with different drive states) rather than sub-fields of a valence vector. The specific wanting-vs-liking separation in SD-014 is not tested. The study also uses food vs. water as distinguishable rewards -- so the drive state and the reward type are confounded (hunger predicts food; thirst predicts water). A cleaner test for SD-014 would hold reward type constant and vary drive state independently.
mapping.source_context: Rat electrophysiology; dorsal CA1 tetrode recording; two-choice memory task vs. random foraging; alternating food-deprivation and water-deprivation sessions; same spatial environment throughout.

confidence_components.notes: PNAS publication, direct hippocampal recording, within-subject design comparing drive states. Mapping fidelity is good -- this is explicitly about hippocampal spatial representations under different motivational states, which is the right level for SD-014. Transfer risk is low; rat hippocampal place cell literature is among the most reproducible in systems neuroscience.
